steps.reasoning: The authors prepared Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O samples, specifically with nominal compositions such as $Tl_{2}Ca_{1.5}BaCu_{3}O_{8.5+x}$, using the melt-solid reaction method facilitated by the fluidity of molten $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$ [@premise-1, @premise-2, @premise-3].
The resistance of the prepared samples was measured as a function of temperature using the four-probe technique [@premise-4].
The authors observed that the resistance of the Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O samples starts to drop sharply at $\sim 120$ K and reaches zero at $\sim 100$ K.
Applying the definition of onset temperature based on the rapid change in curvature [@premise-5], the authors determined the onset temperature to be 120 K.
For a furnace-cooled sample with nominal composition $Tl_{2}Ca_{1.5}BaCu_{3}O_{8.5+x}$, the authors measured a transition midpoint of 112 K and a zero-resistance temperature of 103 K.

The authors confirmed the bulk nature of the superconductivity through magnetic examinations, observing a sample levitating over a magnetic field (diamagnetic repulsion) and measuring the onset of a.c. susceptibility deviation [@premise-6].
The authors assert that the observed superconductivity is stable and reproducible, distinguishing it from unstable high-temperature transitions reported in other systems.

The authors select $Tl_{2}O_{3}$, CaO, and $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$ as starting materials. $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$ is specifically chosen based on its properties of having the lowest melting point among Ba-Cu oxides [@premise-1] and high fluidity in the molten state [@premise-2], under the assumption that melt-solid reactions take place more completely than typical solid-state reactions [@assumption-3].

A sample with the nominal composition $Tl_{2}Ca_{1.5}BaCu_{3}O_{8.5+x}$ is prepared by mixing the starting materials, pressing them into a pellet, heating to 880–910 °C for 3–5 minutes in flowing oxygen, and subsequently furnace-cooling to room temperature.
The resistance of the sample is measured using the standard four-probe technique with silver paste contacts on a rectangular bar cut from the pellet.

The resistance versus temperature plot for the furnace-cooled $Tl_{2}Ca_{1.5}BaCu_{3}O_{8.5+x}$ sample (shown in Fig. 1) indicates that resistance starts to drop sharply at $\sim 120$ K and reaches zero at $\sim 100$ K.
From the resistance data, the authors determine the midpoint of the superconducting transition to be 112 K and the zero-resistance temperature to be 103 K.

The transition temperatures of the $Tl_{2}Ca_{1.5}BaCu_{3}O_{8.5+x}$ sample (112 K midpoint, 103 K zero resistance) are compared with a $Tl_{1.86}CaBaCu_{3}O_{7.8+x}$ sample annealed at 450 °C and a $EuBa_{2}Cu_{3}O_{7-x}$ sample. The authors observe that these values are the highest among the Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O samples prepared in the study and exceed those of high-quality rare-earth-containing samples.
A sample with nominal composition $Tl_{2}Ca_{2}BaCu_{3}O_{9+x}$ is immersed in liquid nitrogen and observed to levitate over a magnetic field of $\sim 5,000$ G.
The authors interpret the observed levitation as a demonstration of strong diamagnetic repulsion [@premise-2].
The authors assert that this strong diamagnetic repulsion is indicative of bulk superconductivity in the samples [@premise-1].
The temperature dependence of the a.c. susceptibility is measured for a sample of nominal composition $Tl_{2}Ca_{1.5}BaCu_{3}O_{8.5+x}$ using the technique of Norton [@premise-3].
The a.c. susceptibility measurements (showing deviation from zero slope) are used to confirm the superconducting nature of the samples.
The authors contrast the effect of annealing at $450^{\circ}\text{C}$ in flowing oxygen on Tl-Ba-Cu-O samples versus Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O samples.

Relying on [@premise-2], the authors identify that this annealing process depresses or destroys the superconducting transition in Tl-Ba-Cu-O samples (which otherwise have zero resistance above liquid nitrogen temperatures as noted in [@premise-1]).
Relying on [@premise-3], the authors identify that the same annealing process slightly improves the superconductivity of Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O samples.

Based on the contrast between the instability of the Tl-Ba-Cu-O samples [@premise-2] and the stability/improvement of the Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O samples [@premise-3], the authors infer that the addition of calcium to the Tl-Ba-Cu-O system stabilizes the superconducting phase against annealing.

Relying on [@premise-4], the authors note that Tl-Ca-Cu-O samples (without barium) exhibit semiconductor-like behavior down to liquid-nitrogen temperatures, contrasting with the superconducting behavior of the Ba-containing systems.

The authors combine the inferred stabilization effect of calcium, the specific annealing outcomes for both systems, and the non-superconducting nature of the barium-free Tl-Ca-Cu-O system to form the final conclusion.
